title: Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu

seo.title: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim) | Easy Vegetarian Recipe
seo.metaDescription: Learn to make delicious spicy Korean Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim) at home! This easy vegetarian recipe is packed with flavor and perfect for weeknights.
longTailKeywords: how to make Korean Braised Tofu
seo.h1: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim) Recipe
h2Headings: What is Dubu Jorim (Korean Braised Tofu)?
How to Make Dubu Jorim: Step-by-Step Instructions
Tips for Perfect Korean Braised Tofu
seo.imageAlt: A close-up of a bowl of spicy Korean braised tofu (Dubu Jorim) garnished with green onions and sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy this flavorful and easy-to-make spicy Korean Braised Tofu, also known as Dubu Jorim, perfect for a vegetarian meal!

description: Dubu Jorim is a beloved Korean side dish featuring pan-fried tofu simmered in a vibrant, spicy, and savory sauce. This vegetarian recipe offers a delightful balance of sweet, spicy, and umami flavors, making it a perfect accompaniment to any Korean meal or a satisfying main dish with rice. It is a comforting and flavorful way to enjoy tofu, showcasing the depth of Korean culinary traditions.

ingredients.notes: pressed and sliced into 1/2-inch thick pieces
1 finely chopped for sauce, 1 sliced for garnish

instructions.title: Prepare and Press Tofu
instructions.text: Carefully slice the tofu block into 1/2-inch thick pieces. Place the tofu slices between several layers of paper towels or a clean kitchen towel. Place a heavy object on top, such as a cast-iron skillet or cutting board with cans, and press for at least 20-30 minutes to remove excess water. This step is crucial for firm tofu.

instructions.title: Pan-Fry the Tofu
instructions.text: Heat 2 tablespoons of cooking oil in a large non-stick skillet over medium-high heat. Once hot, add the pressed tofu slices in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan. Pan-fry for 3-4 minutes per side, until golden brown and slightly crispy. Remove the fried tofu from the pan and set aside on a plate lined with paper towels to drain excess oil.

instructions.title: Prepare the Spicy Braising Sauce
instructions.text: In a medium bowl, combine the soy sauce, gochujang, gochugaru, minced garlic, finely chopped green onion (from one stalk), sesame oil, sugar or corn syrup, and water or vegetable broth. Whisk all the ingredients together until well combined and the sugar is dissolved, creating a smooth, vibrant sauce.

instructions.title: Simmer Tofu in Sauce
instructions.text: Arrange the pan-fried tofu slices in a single layer in a clean, shallow pan or the same skillet used for frying (wiped clean if necessary). Pour the prepared spicy braising sauce evenly over the tofu. Ensure all tofu pieces are coated.

instructions.title: Braise the Tofu
instructions.text: Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Once simmering, reduce the heat to low, cover the pan, and let the tofu braise for 15-20 minutes. Periodically spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure even coating and flavor absorption. The sauce will thicken slightly as it reduces.

instructions.title: Garnish and Serve
instructions.text: Once the tofu has absorbed the flavors and the sauce has thickened to your desired consistency, remove from heat. Garnish generously with the remaining sliced green onions and toasted sesame seeds. Serve immediately with warm rice and other Korean banchan side dishes.

tips: To achieve the best texture, always press your tofu thoroughly. This removes excess water, allowing the tofu to absorb more flavor from the sauce and achieve a firmer, chewier consistency when pan-fried.

Adjust the spice level by modifying the amount of gochugaru (Korean chili flakes). For a milder dish, reduce it or omit it entirely. For extra heat, add more. You can also add a pinch of black pepper for a different kind of warmth.

Dubu Jorim tastes even better the next day as the tofu has more time to soak up the delicious sauce. Store leftovers in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days. Reheat gently on the stovetop or in the microwave.
article:
Dubu Jorim: A Spicy & Savory Korean Vegetarian Delight

In the vibrant tapestry of Korean cuisine, where bold flavors and comforting textures reign supreme, Dubu Jorim holds a special, cherished place. This deceptively simple yet profoundly flavorful dish, often referred to as Spicy Braised Tofu, is a testament to the power of plant-based ingredients transformed into something truly extraordinary. Whether you're a seasoned enthusiast of Korean food or just beginning your culinary exploration, Dubu Jorim offers a delightful journey for your taste buds, proving that vegetarian dishes can be both hearty and incredibly satisfying.

Dubu Jorim is more than just a side dish; it's a staple banchan (small side dish) that frequently graces Korean tables, often serving as a protein-rich main course for vegetarians and vegans. It’s a dish that beautifully balances heat, sweetness, and umami, making it an irresistible accompaniment to a bowl of warm rice.

## What is Dubu Jorim (Korean Braised Tofu)?

Dubu Jorim translates literally to "tofu braised in sauce." At its core, it's firm tofu simmered in a rich, savory, and spicy gochugaru (Korean chili powder)-based sauce until it absorbs all the delicious flavors and achieves a tender, melt-in-your-mouth consistency. The beauty of this Korean Braised Tofu lies in its simplicity and the depth of flavor it achieves with relatively few ingredients.

The process typically involves pan-frying the tofu first to give it a slight crust and firm up its texture, preventing it from crumbling during braising. This also creates more surface area for the sauce to cling to, enhancing the overall taste experience. The braising liquid, a harmonious blend of soy sauce, gochugaru, garlic, sesame oil, and often a touch of sugar, penetrates the tofu, infusing it with a symphony of Korean flavors. It’s a dish that’s both comforting and invigorating, offering a perfect balance for any meal.

## Ingredients for Spicy Braised Tofu

Creating authentic Dubu Jorim requires a few key Korean pantry staples, alongside fresh, high-quality tofu. Here’s what you’ll need:

*   **Tofu:** One block (14-16 oz) of extra-firm or firm tofu is ideal. Ensure it's well-pressed to remove excess water, which allows it to better absorb the braising sauce.
*   **For Frying the Tofu:** A neutral cooking oil (like vegetable or canola oil).
*   **The Braising Sauce:** This is where the magic happens!
    *   **Gochugaru (Korean Chili Flakes):** The star of the show, providing the signature heat and vibrant red color. Adjust to your spice preference.
    *   **Soy Sauce:** For a salty, umami base.
    *   **Garlic:** Freshly minced garlic is essential for depth of flavor.
    *   **Green Onions (Scallions):** Used in the sauce and as a garnish for freshness and a mild oniony bite.
    *   **Sesame Oil:** Adds a nutty aroma and a distinct Korean flavor.
    *   **Sugar or Sweetener:** Balances the spice and salt, adding a touch of sweetness (e.g., sugar, mirin, or corn syrup).
    *   **Water or Vegetable Broth:** The liquid base for the braising process.
    *   **Optional:** A small amount of gochujang (Korean chili paste) for extra depth and a fermented kick, or a pinch of black pepper.

## How to Make Dubu Jorim: Step-by-Step Instructions

Making this delicious Korean Braised Tofu is a straightforward process that yields incredibly rewarding results.

**Step 1: Prepare the Tofu**
First, press your tofu. Wrap the block in paper towels or a clean kitchen towel and place something heavy on top (like a cutting board with cans) for at least 30 minutes, or up to an hour. This removes excess water, preventing soggy tofu and allowing it to absorb the sauce better. Once pressed, slice the tofu into rectangular pieces, about 1/2 inch thick.

**Step 2: Pan-Fry the Tofu**
Heat 2-3 tablespoons of cooking oil in a non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Carefully place the tofu slices in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan. Fry for 3-5 minutes per side, until golden brown and slightly crispy. This step is crucial for texture and preventing the tofu from falling apart. Remove the fried tofu and set aside.

**Step 3: Prepare the Braising Sauce**
In a bowl, combine the gochugaru, soy sauce, minced garlic, finely chopped green onions (reserve some for garnish), sesame oil, sugar, and water or vegetable broth. Stir well until all ingredients are thoroughly mixed and the sugar has dissolved.

**Step 4: Braise the Tofu**
In the same pan (or a clean one if needed), add the fried tofu back in a single layer. Pour the prepared braising sauce evenly over the tofu. Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer, then reduce the heat to medium-low. Let it braise, uncovered, for 10-15 minutes, or until the sauce has thickened and reduced, and the tofu has absorbed the rich flavors. Gently flip the tofu halfway through if desired to ensure even coating.

**Step 5: Garnish and Serve**
Once the sauce has reduced to your desired consistency, remove the pan from the heat. Garnish generously with the reserved chopped green onions and a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds. Serve immediately with warm rice and other Korean side dishes.

## Tips for Perfect Korean Braised Tofu

Achieving the perfect Dubu Jorim is simple with a few insider tips:

*   **Don't Skip Pressing:** This is the most crucial step for texture. Well-pressed tofu won't be watery and will soak up the sauce beautifully.
*   **Fry for Flavor and Texture:** Pan-frying the tofu before braising not only gives it a pleasing exterior but also adds a layer of flavor and prevents it from becoming mushy during the simmer.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** The sauce is the heart of the dish. Taste it before adding to the tofu and adjust the sweetness, spiciness, or saltiness to your preference.
*   **Patience in Braising:** Allow the tofu to simmer gently. This slow cooking process enables the tofu to fully absorb the complex flavors of the sauce. Don't rush it.
*   **Quality Ingredients:** Use good quality gochugaru and soy sauce. The quality of these staples significantly impacts the final flavor of your Korean Braised Tofu.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Dubu Jorim is incredibly versatile. It shines as a standalone vegetarian main dish, particularly when served with a generous bowl of steamed white or multi-grain rice. Its spicy and savory notes perfectly complement the mildness of the rice.

As a banchan, it pairs wonderfully with almost any Korean meal. Consider serving it alongside:

*   **Kimchi:** The fermented, spicy cabbage offers a delightful contrast.
*   **Other Banchan:** Such as seasoned spinach (sigeumchi namul) or bean sprout salad (kongnamul muchim).
*   **Clear Soups:** A light seaweed soup (miyeokguk) or a simple egg drop soup provides a refreshing counterpoint to the rich flavors of the tofu.
*   **Grilled Meats:** If you’re not strictly vegetarian, it makes an excellent side to dishes like bulgogi or galbi.

It’s also an excellent option for meal prep, as the flavors often deepen and meld even more wonderfully after a day or two in the refrigerator.

## Variations & Dietary Adaptations

Dubu Jorim is naturally vegetarian and vegan, making it an excellent choice for a wide range of dietary preferences. However, you can easily adapt it further:

*   **Less Spicy:** Reduce the amount of gochugaru or omit gochujang entirely if you prefer a milder flavor. You can also rinse the tofu slightly after braising to reduce some surface spice.
*   **Gluten-Free:** Simply substitute regular soy sauce with tamari or a certified gluten-free soy sauce.
*   **Extra Umami:** For an even deeper umami flavor, add rehydrated dried shiitake mushrooms to the braising sauce, or use mushroom broth instead of water.
*   **Vegetable Boost:** Feel free to add other vegetables to the braising pan. Sliced onions, zucchini, bell peppers, or additional mushrooms can be added along with the tofu to cook in the flavorful sauce.
*   **Nut-Free:** Dubu Jorim is typically nut-free, but always check your soy sauce and other packaged ingredients if you have severe allergies.

Dubu Jorim is more than just a dish; it's a celebration of simple ingredients transformed into a culinary masterpiece. This Korean Braised Tofu offers a comforting, spicy, and deeply satisfying experience that appeals to vegetarians and meat-eaters alike. With its ease of preparation and incredible flavor profile, it's a must-try for anyone looking to explore the rich and diverse world of Korean cuisine. So, gather your ingredients, follow these steps, and prepare to fall in love with this iconic Korean vegetarian delight.
